Link to Dataset (Building Inventory)

Link to Python Notebook

Plot #1

In this first plot, I am visualizing the unique number of cities in which each agency owns buildings. This allows us to examine the spread (location-wise) of building ownership across each of the different agencies described in the given building inventory dataset. The visualization is important in better understanding which agencies own more buildings across more cities in the US, providing insights into their respective influences nationally. In terms of encoding types, the x-axis is the agency name and is represented as nominal data, while the y-axis is the number of distinct cities and is represented as quantitative data. These encoding choices were made because the different agencies are discrete yet unordered values, and the city count is best represented as a number value. Since there was one nominal and one quantitative field being depicted, I chose to use a bar chart, with each bar representing one agency and the height of the bar representing the distinct city count of that agency. Regarding the color scheme, I chose to color based on the agency name (i.e., based on the bars) to create a clearer, more aesthetic visualization and to help with distinguishing the different agencies/bars. No specific color map/scheme was used since coloring of the bars was solely based on the name of the agency, not any other value/field from the dataset. Regarding data transformations, I first grouped the original dataset by the agency name, then I aggregated by the unique count of cities per agency name. Additionally, I did not set the agency name to be an index of this new dataframe because I wanted the agency name to remain as a separate column for subsequent use in building the altair bar chart. Upon creating the chart, I used a transform filter function such that only agencies with buildings in at least 10 distinct cities were included. This filter was used to remove the agencies that had none/only a few distinct cities and to prevent the chart from looking too cluttered (i.e., from having too many bars). Lastly, a bit of interactivity was incorporated via specifying the tooltips for the bars in the chart, along with the general pan/zoom functions. The tooltips reiterate the name of the agency that is depicted by each bar and notes the number of distinct cities for each bar. The city count is especially useful to include in the tooltips since these values are not directly specified in the bar chart itself (i.e., we can only estimate the values for each bar, so having tooltips provides more specificity for such values).

Plot #2

The second plot depicts the average square footage of the buildings for each year of construction and for different building usage purposes. This visualization is useful for understanding and comparing the trends in general square footage over time (i.e., general rates of building construction) across each usage type, allowing us to see which types of buildings saw more construction in given years. For the chart's encoding types, the x-field is the year of construction and is represented as temporal data (since year is a form of time), while the y-field is the average square footage and is represented as quantitative data (since this is a numerical value). For the x-axis, I chose to set the scale's zero parameter to False to allow the plot to more specifically "zoom in" on the years that actually displayed data for a given usage description, since I had noticed that several usage descriptions did not have data for certain years. A line chart was chosen to most clearly depict trends in the average square footage over a period of time. In terms of coloring variables/color schemes, I chose not to use one because the visualization is already separated by usage description, and so color is not needed to distinguish between different lines. In other words, the data is clear, even without the use of color, and so adding a coloring variable would unnecessarily result in a distracting chart. Minimal data transformations were needed for this plot - I simply chose to exclude entries in the dataset that had a usage description of either "Public" or "Not provided" because, upon examining the chart for those descriptions, I found that there were no significant linear trends (i.e., the plot was blank) for those. Interactivity was incorporated by the inclusion of a drop-down menu that allows users to select the usage description they wish to see in the plot. Options for the drop-down menu included all of the unique usages found in the dataset, excluding "Public" and "Not provided" (for the previously stated reasons), and the default value was set to "Assembly". Upon selection of a usage description, the chart would update (via a transform filter function) to filter the data, only including rows from the dataset that have the selected usage description in the "Usage Description" field. I specifically chose this interactive feature because simply displaying all of the lines for all of the usage descriptions would create a messy appearance, even if I had colored the lines by usage description. Separating the lines for each usage description creates a much clearer visualization and allows users to look at different usages one by one. This also makes the visualization more interesting and dynamic for viewers, allowing for easy navigation between different line charts. Additionally, I kept the standard pan/zoom interactivity so that users can hone in on specific years that they are interested in.
